text: The integration test `tests/test_opencode.py::TestRunResumeIntegration::test_resume_session_produces_contextual_reply` is flaky by nature — it asserts the LLM remembers a 'zorkmid-purple' secret across a resumed session. Real LLM behavior varies (refusals, paraphrasing) so the substring check ('zorkmid' or 'purple') sometimes misses. Re-running usually passes. If it becomes a CI problem, loosen the assertion to just check that msg2 is non-empty and contextually plausible, or replace the prompt with something the model is more reliably willing to echo back.

Symphony deliberately keeps the agent ignorant of Linear. The agent's final assistant message on a turn becomes a Linear comment; Linear comment replies are fed back as the agent's next user message. The agent has no Linear tool, no awareness of ticket state, no special prompt structure for asking questions. This 'dumb pipe' shape was chosen because (a) it kept the integration surface tiny and language/agent-agnostic, (b) it removed the need to design agent-side tool protocols for ask-human/post-comment, and (c) it made comment concatenation (when the user posts multiple replies between polls) trivial: just glue them with author/timestamp headers and feed as one user message. The cost is that the agent can't ever 'know' it's on Linear, which means it can't reference ticket metadata or other tickets unless we shove them into the prompt explicitly. If someone ever wants the agent to manipulate Linear state directly (e.g. close the ticket, attach a PR link), they would need to break this abstraction — adding an MCP/tool on the agent side and giving the daemon a delegation model. Don't add this casually.

We explicitly rejected the WORKFLOW.md-style configurable prompt template approach that the openai/symphony SPEC.md describes. The system prompt and ticket prompt format are hardcoded in the orchestrator. Reasoning: this tool is built for solo personal use with stable workflow needs; adding a YAML-front-matter + Liquid-template loader and a file watcher would have multiplied the complexity for no concrete benefit at MVP scope. If users start wanting per-repo prompt customization, the cleanest reintroduction is to look for a `.symphony/prompt.md` in the repo at bootstrap time and use it instead of the hardcoded one. Don't bring back hot-reload or front-matter unless there's a real second use case driving it.

QA-on-missing-workspace deliberately does not auto-reclone. When _reconcile_serve picks a winner whose workspace was deleted (typical cause: the ticket was previously moved out of an active state, which triggers cleanup), we comment+transition out of QA rather than running prepare() to clone fresh.

Reason: the agent never git-pushes (sandbox hides credentials by design — see AGENTS.md). The agent's commits therefore live ONLY in the local workspace clone. If we re-cloned for QA, _git_switch_branch would fall through to 'git switch -c' against the remote default branch, and .symphony/serve would silently run against base-branch code — i.e. QA would 'start' but serve the wrong thing. Refusing to start is the safer failure.

The supported flow is to QA *before* moving the ticket to Done. Done→QA isn't recoverable in the current architecture.

This would only become a sensible feature if push-from-agent becomes the norm (so the branch is reachable from the remote). The user has separately mentioned wanting Symphony to optionally not auto-create per-ticket branches (push target/creds/PR being out of Symphony's scope) — that change, if it lands, may revisit this decision.

We removed the `opencode.model` config option (was at `AppConfig.opencode.model`). The reasoning: OpenCode's own configuration already selects a model, and in the workflows people actually run — multi-step agentic loops, tool use, code edits — overriding the model from Symphony's outer layer doesn't add value. The option was effectively dead config surface.

Don't reintroduce a model selector at the Symphony level without a concrete use case. If model choice ever needs to vary per-ticket (e.g. cheap model for triage, strong model for implementation), the right place is OpenCode's config or a per-project setting, not a single global string in Symphony's config.

The removal was a breaking change (`feat!:`); existing configs with an `opencode:` section fail loudly at load via pydantic's `extra="forbid"`.

WebhookServer injects on_wake by creating a per-server _HandlerWithWake subclass with on_wake as a class attribute. Calling it via self._on_wake() binds 'self' as the first arg (because Python treats functions stored on a class as methods); to invoke the raw callable, use type(self)._on_wake() in BaseHTTPRequestHandler subclasses. This pattern is also used for _webhook_secret (set as a server instance attribute, accessed via self.server._webhook_secret in the handler). If the injection pattern is refactored, audit both call sites — the current shape is slightly unusual but minimal.

Daemon-posted comments are tagged with a visible Markdown footer of the form `*Symphony · {kind}*`, appended at the tracker-adapter layer (linear_tracker.py / github_tracker.py) from a `kind` string supplied by the caller (e.g. "workspace", "final", "error", "context: 37,074 tokens"). Detection uses the helper `is_bot_comment(body)` in tracker.py, which checks for the substring `*Symphony · ` (middle dot is U+00B7). This replaced the old HTML-comment sentinel (`<!-- symphony:bot -->`) because Linear's renderer does not strip HTML comments, making the previous sentinel visible and ugly in the UI.

Two alternatives were considered and rejected this round:

1. Invisible zero-width characters as a sentinel (ZWSP / ZWNJ, etc.). Rejected as hacky, fragile, and prone to leaking via copy/paste into human comments.

2. Filtering by token-owner user id via `viewer { id }`. Rejected because when running with a personal API token, this would silently drop comments typed by the same human whose token is used, causing the daemon to ignore genuine human input.

The marker approach also removes the need for a separate bot user identity (the previous current_user_id / bot_user_id / bot_user_email machinery was all dropped), letting users run the daemon with their own personal API token.

Setup-error retry loop fires off historical comments when last_seen_comment_id is None. _save_setup_error (and the ProjectConfigError branch of _resume_pipeline) used to persist last_seen_comment_id=None when the error comment couldn't be posted to the tracker. On the next poll tick, _has_new_human_comment(tid, None) returns all comments, so any historical human comment satisfied the 'new comment' gate and re-fired _new_ticket_pipeline every poll interval — symptoms: workspace metadata comment posted multiple times, 'New ticket pipeline starting' repeating every poll. Fix establishes a three-tier baseline hierarchy at both error-save sites: (1) error_comment.id if posted, (2) existing last_seen_comment_id if non-None, (3) most-recent comment id via Tracker.list_comments_since(tid, None) with a graceful None fallback on tracker failure. Whenever you add a new error path that sets setup_error or transitions to failed, preserve this invariant: last_seen_comment_id must never be None when comments exist on the ticket.
